content.full_text:
CITY OF CONCORD
1950 Parkside Drive, MS/08
Concord, California 94519-2578
FAX: (510) 798-0636

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY
Telephone: (510) 671-3160

Michael D. Martello
City Attorney

Laurel Reiterman Weil
Assistant City Attorney

Mark T. Boehme
Deputy City Attorney

Concord

September 1, 1992


Mr. Wayne Ordos
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: REQUEST FOR ADVICE

Dear Mr. Ordos:

On behalf of Vice Mayor Mark DeSaulnier, this office requests formal written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) with respect to his obligations under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. Our question is whether Vice Mayor DeSaulnier may participate in certain decisions relating to a downtown redevelopment project in the City of Concord.

One of the decisions is scheduled for consideration by the City Council/Redevelopment Agency on October 6, 1992. We would therefore appreciate your expedited consideration of this request. Please contact me if you need any additional information.

BACKGROUND

1. The Project.

The project, known as Concord Cinema Square, is located in Concord's downtown redevelopment project area and includes a ten screen movie theater complex, 40,000 square feet of commercial retail/office space and 28 apartment units. The project is expected to attract 800,000 visitors yearly to the downtown area.

The project, as approved, is a joint venture between the Concord Redevelopment Agency ("Agency") and the Developer, Oliver McMillan Cinema Square, L.P. the Agency owns the land and will own and operate the parking facilities. The Developer, Oliver McMillan Cinema Square, L.P., will develop the balance of the project. The Agency approved the Development Plan and entered into a Development and Disposition Agreement ("DDA") with the Developer on June 15, 1991. Section 2.03 of the DDA provides, in relevant part, as follows:

Mr. Wayne Ordos
September 1, 1992
Re:    Request for Advice

Page 2


Prior to the date of this Agreement, the Agency and Developer jointly developed
the Development Plan attached hereto as Exhibit D. By execution of this
Agreement, the agency and developer hereby approves [sic] the
Development Plan. The Development Plan calls for development on the Property
of the Developer Improvements and Agency Improvements. . .

The proposed design for the project has not been substantially modified since the DDA
was executed. No other major changes to the DDA of the project have been made since that
time.

Since the Agency approved the project and the DDA was executed, both the Agency and
the Developer have proceeded with the steps set forth in the DDA for carrying ut the project.
These include various requirements for the Agency approval of specific items. Our question,
and the basis for this request, is whether Vice Mayor DeSaulnier may participate in these types
of decisions.

All of the Agency decisions on the Construction Plans, the Financing Plan and other
similar items are governed by objective criteria set forth in the DDA. For example, Section
2.04 of the DDA provides in pertinent part:

The Agency shall, if the Construction Plans submitted conform to the provisions
of this Agreement, the approved Development Plan and the Redevelopment Plan, and
exhibit a high quality design, approve in writing such Construction Plans and no further
filing by the Developer or approval by the Agency thereof shall be required except with
respect to any material change.

Similarly, Section 2.05 of the DDA provides, in pertinent part:

Developer shall submit to the Agency its Financing Plan for financing of the
construction of the improvements. The Financing Plan shall be submitted no later than
November 1, 1991 [dater later revised to September 21, 1992] . . . . . the
Agency's review of the Financing Plan shall be limited to determining if the
contemplated financing will reasonably be available, will provide sufficient funds
for the construction of the Developer Improvements and Agency Improvements,
will be provided on terms consistent with the terms and conditions of this
Agreement and, in the case of loan financing, will provide for the lender to have
recourse to the general partners in [sic] Developer in the event of default on the
loan.

Mr. Wayne Ordos
September 1, 1992
Re: Request for Advice

Page 3


Related to the issue of the Financing Plan approval is the question of whether the
Agency's fee interest in the real property will be subordinated to the construction loan obtained
by the Developer. The DDA includes a general provision that the Agency's fee interest will not
be subordinated to any lien, mortgage, deed of trust, etc. Put simply, given the current lending
market, the lender wants a first deed of trust as security for the loan. Notwithstanding the DDA
provision, in February 1992 the Agency (1) authorized Developer to submit a loan application
to U.S. Bancorp that included a subordination component; and (2) made a $100,000 deposit to
facilitate the processing of the loan, reserving to the Agency the future consideration of the
subordination issue.

The Financing Plan (which will be submitted for approval to the Agency for action at its
October 6, 1992 meeting) will include the U.S. Bancorp loan which may provide for the
Agency's subordination of its fee interest. At that time, the Council will also consider an
amendment to the DDA that would formally authorize the Agency subordination.

2. Mr. DeSaulnier's Business.

Vice Mayor DeSaulnier owns and operates a restaurant located diagonally across a six-
lane intersection from the Cinema Square project. He does not own the property on which his
restaurant is located but is on a long-term (7 years remaining), fixed rent lease. The restaurant's
annual gross sales are approximately $1 million.

Within an approximately four-square block area surrounding the project, there are
approximately 54 restaurants ranging from sit-down establishments to fast food restaurants. The
project itself includes 4 restaurants and an ice cream store. In addition, the theater will include
the typical theater food concessions (popcorn, sodas, hotdogs).

The estimated 800,000 persons who will be attracted into the area by the theater complex
translates to approximately 2,200 persons per day. This additional traffic in the area
theoretically increases the number of potential customers for all retail businesses in the area
including the restaurants. However, the project tenants include new food establishments which
will compete with the 50+ nearby restaurants for their existing business as well as the potential
new business. Thus it is possible that there will be no discernible effect on the nearby
restaurants.

Mr. Wayne Ordos
September 1, 1992
Re: Request for Advice

Page 4

QUESTIONS AND DISCUSSION

We understand that, given Mr. DeSaulnier's business and leasehold interests, the applicable FPPC materiality regulations are Sections 18702.2(g) and 18702.4.¹ Since the real property lease if long-term and provides for a fixed rent, it does not appear that any of the project decisions could have any effect on the value of the lease. Thus the only question is whether any of the upcoming decisions on the project would affect the annualized gross revenues of the restaurant by $10,000 or more.

Since the project was approved before Mr. DeSaulnier assumed office in November, 1991, five months after the DDA was executed, it appears unnecessary to determine the impact of the entire project on Mr. DeSaulnier's business unless there will be a particular Agency decision which relates to the reconsideration of the entire project by the Agency or the Developer.

In addition, to the extent that the remaining decisions on the project implement the requirements in the DDA or are based on disqualification analysis, these decisions are severable from the earlier decisions approving the project. It would therefore seem to be appropriate to analyze these decisions individually to determine whether there would be a material financial effect on Mr. DeSaulnier's business.

Based on the foregoing facts and discussion, we ask whether Vice Mayor DeSaulnier is disqualified from voting on the Agency decisions to approve the Financing Plan, to approve the Construction Plans, etc.

Please contact me if you need additional information in your consideration of this request. Thank you for your time and attention.

Sincerely,

[signature]

MICHAEL D. MARTELLO
City Attorney

MDM/cls

¹ Since Mr. DeSaulnier does not own the real property, we understand that the so-called "300 foot" rule in FPPS Regulation, Section 18702.3 (a) (1) does not apply.


[illegible]

Law Offices of

OLSON
CONNELLY
HAGEL
FONG &
LEIDIGH

September 14, 1992


John Wallace, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE:  PENDING ADVICE REQUEST FROM MICHAEL MARTELLO,
     CONCORD CITY ATTORNEY
     YOUR FILE NO. 92-471

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     This firm has been retained to advise Oliver
McMillan Cinema Square, L.P., in connection with the
above pending request from Michael Martello, Concord
City Attorney, made on behalf of Vice Mayor Mark
DeSaulnier.  On behalf of our client, we would like to
provide the following additional background information
and documents for your consideration in this matter.
My client has discussed this with the City Attorney,
and he does not object to our making this submission.

     As stated in Mr. Martello's letter to your agency,
the Concord Cinema Square Project is a joint venture
between the Concord Redevelopment Agency and our
client.  The terms and conditions of this joint venture
are set forth in a Development and Disposition
Agreement ("DDA") which was entered into by both
parties on June 15, 1991.  I have enclosed a copy of
the original Agreement for your review.

     Since June 15, 1991, no major changes to the
project have been made, and only a few minor amendments
have been made to the Agreement for the purpose of
extending the deadlines for the submission of various
plans; these are not enclosed.  I will provide copies
of the amendments at your request.  In all major
aspects, the Agreement remains the same to this day.

     We also enclose a copy of the minutes of the
Redevelopment Agency meeting of February 28, 1992.
These minutes clarify the issue raised in
Mr. Martello's letter concerning the subordination of
the Agency's fee interest.  See page 3, paragraph 1 of
Mr. Martello's letter.  It may appear from Mr.
Martello's letter that one issue before the Agency on

LANCE H. OLSON
BRUCE J. HAGEL
LEROY Y. FONG
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH
GEORGE WARTERS
DIANE M. FISHBURN

Of Counsel
LLOYD G. CONNELLY

CapitolBank Center
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

Telephone (916) 442-2952
Facsimile (916) 442-1280

John Wallace, Esq.
September 14, 1992
Page 2


October 6, 1992 is whether the Agency will subordinate its fee
interest in the property to the construction lender for the
project. However, as the enclosed minutes indicate, this
decision was made by the Agency on February 28, 1992 when it
committed $100,000 for the purpose of obtaining U.S. Bancorp's
commitment to make the construction loan.

While the original DDA itself did not authorize the
subordination of the agency's fee interest, both the Agency and
our client contemplated at the time that construction financing
of the project would likely require such subordination, and that,
if required by the lender, the Agency would agree to such
subordination.

At the February 28 meeting, the Agency considered the U.S.
Bancorp financing proposal for the construction financing of the
Project. The minutes indicate that the financing proposal
included a subordination condition by the lender and that this
item was thoroughly discussed by the Agency members.

At the close of its deliberations, the Agency authorized by
a 4-0 vote the submission of the U.S. Bancorp loan application
with the subordination condition and the payment of $100,000 from
Agency funds for the loan processing fee. The minutes also
indicate that, while the Agency agreed to the subordination of
its fee interest to the construction lender, it did reserve the
question of subordination with respect to the future permanent or
take-out financing which will occur after the project is built
out. There are no decisions pending before the Agency on the
question of future subordination.

U.S. Bancorp recently approved the loan application for the
construction financing, and the pending decision on October 6,
1992 is for formal Agency approval of the Construction Financing
Plan which includes the U.S. Bancorp loan and of conforming
amendments to the DDA. The Agency's discretion in making this
decision is spelled out in the DDA in Section 2.05. (pp. 11-12 of
the DDA) and quoted in the City Attorney's letter to your agency.

Thank you for your time and attention to this matter.
Please feel free to contact me if you would like any additional
information or would like to discuss this matter further. We
also join in the City Attorney's request for your expedited

John Wallace, Esq.
September 14, 1992
Page 3

response to the advice request based on the upcoming October 6,
1992 consideration of this matter.

Very truly yours,

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

[signature]
DIANE M. FISHBURN

DMF:jlh
Enclosures
cc: Michael Martello, Esq.
    Mr. Morgan Dene Oliver
